cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and people between the Eastern and Western hemispheres following Columbus's 1492 voyage, which permanently transformed global ecologies and population distributions.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A Spanish colonial labor system that granted conquistadors the right to compel indigenous people to work in mines or on plantations in exchange for Christian education, effectively creating forced labor.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A form of slavery in which enslaved people are treated as property to be bought, sold, and inherited, with no rights — the system that developed in the Atlantic world using primarily African labor on New World plantations.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The three-cornered Atlantic trading network in which European manufactured goods went to Africa, enslaved Africans went to the Americas, and American agricultural products (sugar, tobacco, cotton) went to Europe.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The economic theory that a nation's wealth depends on accumulating bullion (gold and silver) through a favorable balance of trade — colonies existed to supply raw materials and serve as markets for the home country.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A business entity in which multiple investors pool capital and share profits and risks, used by European states to fund expensive overseas trading ventures — the Dutch and British East India Companies are the most famous examples.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The catastrophic 80–95% population decline of indigenous Americans in the century after 1492, caused primarily by epidemic diseases (smallpox, measles, typhus) to which they had no prior immunity.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The Spanish colonial racial hierarchy that categorized people by European, African, and indigenous ancestry — categories included peninsulares, creoles, mestizos, and mulatos.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Spanish-born individuals who held the highest status in the colonial casta hierarchy — they monopolized the most powerful government and church positions in Spanish America.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Individuals born in the Americas to two Spanish parents — they had significant wealth and social status but were excluded from the highest political offices reserved for peninsulares.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Individuals of mixed European and indigenous American ancestry in the Spanish colonial casta system.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Individuals of mixed European and African ancestry in the Spanish colonial casta system.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The Portuguese strategy of establishing fortified trading posts (feitorias) at strategic coastal locations rather than conquering large inland territories — this allowed control of maritime trade routes with relatively small military forces.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Gold and silver, especially the vast amounts extracted from Spanish American mines (Potosí, New Spain) and shipped to Europe, which fueled the price revolution and European global trade.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A contract labor system used after the abolition of slavery (and in some colonial contexts before) in which workers signed multi-year contracts to work in exchange for passage — used especially to bring South Asian and Chinese laborers to Caribbean and Pacific plantations.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A small, highly maneuverable Portuguese sailing ship with triangular lateen sails that could sail closer to the wind than square-rigged ships — it enabled Portuguese exploration of the African coast and Atlantic.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A navigation instrument that measures the angle between celestial objects and the horizon, allowing sailors to determine their latitude — essential for open-ocean navigation.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A large, heavily armed Spanish or Portuguese ocean-going sailing ship used for trade and warfare across the Atlantic and Pacific — larger and more cargo-capable than the caravel.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A large Dutch merchant sailing ship designed to maximize cargo capacity and minimize crew — it gave the Dutch a cost advantage in the global carrying trade in the seventeenth century.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A large hacienda-style Spanish colonial agricultural estate worked by indigenous or enslaved labor, producing cash crops (sugar, tobacco, cacao) for export.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The Andean labor draft system adapted by Spanish colonizers to compel indigenous workers to labor in silver mines, especially at Potosí — workers could die from cave-ins and mercury poisoning.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: He sponsored the 1492 voyage that first linked the Western and Eastern hemispheres, sailing west under Spanish patronage in search of a direct route to Asia — his voyages triggered the era of Spanish colonization.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: His 1519–1522 expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the Earth, demonstrating the globe's true scale and the feasibility of transoceanic routes — he died in the Philippines but his crew returned to Spain.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: In 1498, he established the first direct European sea route to India by sailing around the Cape of Good Hope, bypassing Ottoman-controlled overland trade routes and enabling Portuguese dominance of Indian Ocean trade.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A Portuguese prince who sponsored systematic exploration of the West African coast in the early fifteenth century, establishing Portugal's early lead in maritime exploration and the Atlantic slave trade infrastructure.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: This transfer of species between hemispheres after 1492 permanently transformed global agriculture and population: European diseases wiped out 80–95% of indigenous Americans, while American crops (potatoes, tomatoes, maize) eventually doubled European and Asian populations.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The founding of the Dutch East India Company (1602) and British East India Company (1600) — these royally chartered, investor-owned monopoly companies conquered and administered vast Asian territories, transforming trade companies into proto-colonial states.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The forced migration of approximately 12.5 million Africans to the Americas between 1500 and 1900, creating new diaspora communities and permanently transforming both African demographics and New World labor systems.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Spain's extraction of massive quantities of silver from Andean mines (especially Potosí) using mita forced labor — this silver flowed through Spain to China (which demanded silver for tax payments), fueling global trade but devastating indigenous mining communities.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A syncretism of West African religious practices (Yoruba traditions) with Catholicism that emerged among enslaved Africans in Cuba — one of several Afro-Caribbean religions that demonstrate how enslaved people preserved cultural identity.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A religion originating in West Africa (especially among the Fon people of Dahomey) that was transplanted to the Caribbean through the slave trade and blended with Catholicism — an example of cultural resistance and syncretism.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Precious metal (gold and silver) used as currency or currency backing — Spain's extraction of American silver from Potosí and New Spain flooded European markets and caused the 'Price Revolution' of the sixteenth century.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: A business organization granted exclusive rights to trade in a particular region by a royal charter — the Dutch VOC and British EIC are prime examples; these companies could make treaties, wage war, and administer territories.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: This 1831 rebellion in Virginia, led by an enslaved preacher, killed approximately 55 white people before being suppressed — it caused Southern states to tighten restrictions on enslaved people and became a reference point in debates over slavery.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Any form of labor in which workers are compelled by force, debt, law, or coercion rather than free contract — includes encomienda, mita, chattel slavery, and indentured servitude.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why European diseases caused catastrophic mortality among indigenous Americans while having far less effect on European colonizers?

choices.text: Indigenous Americans had no prior exposure to Old World diseases like smallpox, so they lacked acquired immunity, while Europeans had developed partial immunity from centuries of exposure.
Indigenous Americans had weaker immune systems because they had less protein in their diets than Europeans.
Europeans deliberately introduced diseases as biological warfare against indigenous populations.
Indigenous Americans were already weakened by famine before European contact, making them uniquely vulnerable.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The development of joint-stock companies like the Dutch East India Company (VOC) and British East India Company (EIC) in the seventeenth century most directly enabled European commercial expansion by:

choices.text: Spreading the financial risk of expensive long-distance voyages across many investors, making it possible to fund operations that no individual merchant or government could afford alone.
Replacing monarchical authority with democratic governance in European trade zones.
Ensuring that all profits from Asian trade were returned to royal treasuries rather than private merchants.
Creating free-trade zones where no tariffs were charged on goods passing through company-controlled ports.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The casta system in Spanish colonial Americas most directly served which of the following functions?

choices.text: It created a hierarchical social structure based on ancestry that concentrated political and economic power in the hands of European-born colonizers while marginalizing indigenous and African-descended populations.
It was designed to promote intermarriage between European and indigenous populations to create a unified colonial society.
It guaranteed equal rights and land ownership to all categories of colonial subjects regardless of ancestry.
It prohibited any intermarriage between racial categories and successfully maintained pure European bloodlines.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains why the Atlantic slave trade expanded so dramatically after 1600?

choices.text: The labor-intensive plantation crops grown in tropical Americas (sugar, tobacco, cotton) required massive workforces, and the catastrophic decline of indigenous American populations from disease created an acute labor shortage that colonizers filled with enslaved Africans.
European governments decided that African labor was more culturally compatible with plantation agriculture than indigenous American labor.
The Atlantic slave trade expanded because African kingdoms actively sought to sell their citizens to European merchants as part of diplomatic agreements.
The slave trade expanded primarily because it was more profitable than trading spices, replacing the spice trade as the dominant form of Atlantic commerce.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: How did the Columbian Exchange's transfer of New World crops (potatoes, maize, sweet potatoes) to the Eastern Hemisphere most significantly affect global demographics over the long term?

choices.text: New World crops provided high-calorie yields on marginal land unsuitable for traditional grains, enabling population growth in Europe, China, and Africa that eventually contributed to rapid global population increase.
New World crops replaced all traditional Eastern Hemisphere grains within a generation, immediately doubling food production.
New World crops caused famine in Asia by out-competing rice cultivation, reducing Asian populations.
New World crops only affected the Americas because Eastern Hemisphere farmers refused to adopt unfamiliar plants.
